paper_title: CalVerT: Augmenting Agents with Calibrated Verifier Telemetry Improves Action and Learning in Knowledge-Intensive Tasks

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: LLM agents in knowledge intensive question answering take retrieval and reasoning actions with incomplete knowledge about whether their current answer is uncertain, unsupported, or already complete. This produces two failure modes: committing to confident but unsupported answers, which hurts accuracy, and over-retrieving when the evidence in hand already suffices, resulting in wasted compute. To give agents a more complete picture of the state space they are operating in, we introduce calibrated verifier telemetry (CalVerT), which augments the agent's state with additional telemetry: a calibrated self-confidence score and a grounding verifier score. We show that CalVerT can improve agents in both training-free and training-based settings. On four QA benchmarks, we find that CalVerT raises F1 by triggering retrieval in cases where agents over-rely on parametric knowledge, while cutting redundant retrieval in cases where agents have sufficient context to answer. We show that CalVerT can augment existing QA frameworks without training. Moreover, CalVerT also improves trained systems: by simply augmenting an agent's state with telemetry, we observe improvements after reinforcement learning, as compared to an agent with identical training but no CalVerT telemetry.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: CALVERT: Augmenting Agents with Calibrated Verifier Telemetry Improves Action and Learning in Knowledge-Intensive Tasks

sections.title: Ying Ding
sections.content: Elias Stengel-Eskin

sections.title: The University of Texas at Austin
Abstract
sections.content: LLM agents in knowledge intensive question answering take retrieval and reasoning actions with incomplete knowledge about whether their current answer is uncertain, unsupported, or already complete. This produces two failure modes: committing to confident but unsupported answers, which hurts accuracy, and overretrieving when the evidence in hand already suffices, resulting in wasted compute. To give agents more complete picture of the state space they are operating in, we introduce calibrated verifier telemetry (CALVERT), which augments the agents state with additional telemetry: calibrated self-confidence score and grounding verifier score. We show that CALVERT can improve agents in both trainingfree and training-based settings. On four QA benchmarks, we find that CALVERT raises F1 by triggering retrieval in cases where agents over-rely on parametric knowledge, while cutting redundant retrieval in cases where agents have sufficient context to answer. We show that CALVERT can augment existing QA frameworks without training. Moreover, CALVERT also improves trained systems: by simply augmenting an agents state with telemetry, we observe improvements after reinforcement learning, as compared to an agent with identical training but no CALVERT telemetry.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly solve knowledge-intensive question answering (QA) and tool-use tasks by repeatedly retrieving, reflecting, and revising (Yao et al., 2023; Trivedi et al., 2023; Asai et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2024a). Knowing when to perform these actions is critical; however, many current agents fail according to two failure modes: parametric over-trust, where agents over-rely on their potentially faulty parametric knowledge, and over-retrieval, where 1Code: https://github.com/ashwinn-v/CalVerT Figure 1: Exposing calibrated confidence and grounding signals to the agent each turn yields better action choices. Left: without telemetry, the agent retrieves additional evidence but still stops with an unsupported answer for the subquestion, causing an incorrect final answer. Right: CALVERT exposes low confidence and weak grounding in the form of verifier telemetry, so the agent first retrieves when the current evidence is insufficient, then refines the answer for targeted retrieval, and only commits after the answer is well grounded. agents continue retrieving passages after already having sufficient evidence. The former poses risk to answer correctness, while the latter leads to higher latency and cost (though it can also hurt answer quality). We argue that existing agents (Deng et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2024b; Yang et al., 2024; Qin et al., 2024) are often fighting an uphill battle in balancing these concerns: they generally lack the measurement capabilities to know whether the current answer is supported by the evidence in hand or whether the model itself is confident in it. To address this lack of measurement, we introduce Calibrated Verifier Telemetry (CALVERT), which provides agents with measurement telemetry across two turn-level signals: the models calibrated confidence in its current answer, and whether that answer is entailed by the retrieved evidence. Neither signal is sufficient on its own: confidence alone cannot separate confidently wrong answer from an equally confident (but unverified) correct answer, and grounding alone cannot separate cases where the agent needs to retrieve more evidence from those where the evidence it has disagrees with the answer. Fig. 1 shows the resulting failure on one subquestion from the HotpotQA distractor set (Yang et al., 2018) evaluated on Qwen332B (Yang et al., 2025). When no telemetry signals are exposed (left), the agent cannot distinguish plausible but weakly supported extraction from grounded answer, so it stops with the wrong subquestion answer. With CALVERT (right), low confidence triggers additional retrieval, and low grounding on the retrieved claim triggers refinement rather than commitment. The agent commits only after the candidate answer is supported by the retrieved evidence. Specifically, our framework pairs calibrated verbal self-confidence score (Wang and StengelEskin, 2026) with per-claim grounding verifier (Tang et al., 2024), and surfaces both at each turn as telemetry signals. These calibrated scores are added to the agents state before each action (one of {commit, retrieve, refine, decompose}). Crucially, we let the agent incorporate these scores into its decision-making naturally, without defining specific confidence or grounding thresholds. We study two complementary ways to incorporate CALVERT scores. In training-free settings, we simply insert the telemetry signal into the prompt of frozen agent, thereby steering it toward targeted retrieval or refinement instead of committing to the current action when the available evidence is insufficient. We demonstrate the flexibility and performance of this approach across four QA benchmarks, with the same signal porting to five existing retrieval frameworks (Self-Ask (Press et al., 2023), TARG (Wang et al., 2026), SUGAR (Zubkova et al., 2025), Verify-and-Edit (Zhao et al., 2023), SeaKR (Yao et al., 2025)). Specifically, it improves F1 scores on four of the five frameworks by up to +15.4% on TARG and +7.8% on SeaKR. We also show that CALVERT can improve trained agents. Specifically, following the reward from Search-R1 (Jin et al., 2025), we train agents backed by open-source models using reinforcement learning (RL). We show that, under identical training conditions, agents with states augmented by CALVERT achieve higher performance than those without, for both Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-30B-A3B. Here, we observe gains of +5.9% F1 for Qwen38B and +3.3% F1 for Qwen3-30B-A3B on the HotpotQA distractor subset.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Existing approaches expose only single signal per turn, either confidence or grounding, but not both, so the agent cannot effectively decide which action to take. Training-free methods drive action choice from one signal at time: fixed schedules (Trivedi et al., 2023), self-reflection (Shinn et al., 2023), entropy thresholds (Jiang et al., 2023), complexity routing (Jeong et al., 2024), iterative decomposition (Press et al., 2023), or single-signal gates (Ding et al., 2026; Zubkova et al., 2025; Yao et al., 2025; Vinod and Erk, 2026). Training-based methods likewise condition on single signal, learning the policy end-to-end via outcome reinforcement learning (RL) (Jin et al., 2025) or via reflection tokens emitted by the policy itself (Asai et al., 2024). Recent work also exposes verbalized uncertainty to agent loops: Agent-BRACE (Singh et al., 2026) trains decoupled belief-state model that summarizes history as verbalized, uncertainty-labeled claims about the environment state, which separately trained policy then conditions on. Unlike this learned belief over latent world state, CALVERTs telemetry is training-free and does not encode belief about the environment, instead exposing two QA-specific external signals about the quality of the agents current answer. Cost-aware retrieval gating. Prior work gates retrieval using single confidence score, either fixed before the trajectory or read per turn (Jiang et al., 2023; Jeong et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2026; Zubkova et al., 2025; Yao et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2023). In contrast, we use calibrated verbal confidence to decide when to retrieve and commit. Multi-hop agent loops. ReAct (Yao et al., 2023), IRCoT (Trivedi et al., 2023), Self-Ask (Press et al., 2023), Self-RAG (Asai et al., 2024), Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023), and LATS (Zhou et al., 2024a) interleave retrieval and reasoning with internal stopping criteria, but do not incorporate external verifier signals. We include calibrated confidence and grounding scores in the agent state before each action so that the agents decision is conditioned on two orthogonal uncertainty signals.

sections.title: 3 Calibrated Verifier Telemetry
sections.content: We incorporate CALVERT into ReAct-style (Yao et al., 2023) agent loop. planner first decomposes the question into subgoal directed acyclic graph (DAG) G, and at each turn, the agent observes the running evidence pool and chooses one action from small discrete vocabulary: {commit, retrieve, refine, decompose}. Commit accepts the current answer, stops acting on the current subgoal, and stores that answer along with its supporting facts in memory for the composer to assemble the final answer; retrieve searches for additional evidence passages; refine regenerates the answer from the existing evidence by using refinement prompt; and decompose gives up on the current subquestion and breaks it into smaller subquestions when retrieval and refinement have not helped. Agents augmented with CALVERT receive telemetry signal in their state representation, informing their subsequent actions. Verifier Telemetry Scores The telemetry is comprised of two channels; each has two values: (1) SELF-CONFIDENCE. The score pair (NVC, SC) [0, 1] is produced by DINCO (Wang and StengelEskin, 2026): NVC is the normalized verbal confidence from prompting the generator with Is this answer correct? Yes/No", and SC is the selfconsistency rate of the generator. (2) GROUNDING FAMILY. separate verifier model (BespokeMiniCheck-7B; Tang et al., 2024) decomposes the candidate answer beam into claims and scores each claims entailment against the current evidence pool E. We extract two values: the mean gmean and the minimum gmin (the worst-grounded claim), with the latter highlighting the weakest link. This results in 4 total telemetry scalars. Agent Loop. The agent operates over question q, an evidence pool E, and subgoal DAG G, produced by the planner. On each turn it picks the next unresolved subgoal G, drafts candidateanswer beam given (n, E), and chooses one of the four actions. The pair (NVC, SC) is computed once when the agent first visits and cached while the agent remains on n; the grounding pair (gmean, gmin) is recomputed every turn over (C, E). The four scalars are provided in the prompt alongside brief natural-language framing of what each channel measures; the prompt encodes no hardcoded thresholds, so the calibrated values are inputs the agent reasons over rather than gates baked into the template. The loop terminates when is fully resolved or the per-question turn budget is exhausted, after which composer pass produces the final answer from (q, E).

sections.title: 4 Experiments and Results
sections.content: Datasets. We evaluate on four open-domain QA benchmarks: three multi-hop datasets HotpotQAdistractor (Yang et al., 2018), 2WikiMultihopQA (Ho et al., 2020), and MuSiQue (Trivedi et al., 2022) and the single hop factoid dataset WiTQA (Maekawa et al., 2024). We sample =300 dev questions per benchmark for evaluation. Retrieval uses BM25 (Robertson and Walker, 1994) over each multi-hop distractor pool, and full-Wikipedia BM25 with cross-encoder reranking for WiTQA. For training-time experiments, we train on 1,600 HotpotQA-distractor questions and evaluate on held-out =200 dev slice. Metrics. We report token-level F1 and normalized exact match (EM). To quantify action efficiency, we report mean agent actions per example (turns/ex) and per-action-type counts. Paired conditions are summarized by telemetry minus no-telemetry deltas (F1, turns/ex).

sections.title: 4.1 Prompt-based Methods
sections.content: We compare two prompt conditions on frozen generator running ReAct-style loop. We have +tel, in which the telemetry scalars and their naturallanguage framing appear in the per-turn prompt, and -tel, in which both are removed while the remaining prompt is held fixed. The planner, retriever, and composer are held fixed across the two. Implementation details are in Appendix E. Setup. We evaluate the prompt level recipe on both generators across all four benchmarks. The three multi-hop benchmarks (HotpotQA-distractor, 2Wiki, MuSiQue) are evaluated on the agent loop; WiTQA uses single-turn factoid loop with binary {closed-book, retrieve-then-answer} decision. The pipeline (planner, MiniCheck verifier, telemetry computation, and composer) is fixed; only the action vocabulary and retrieval index change. Results. Table 1 reports per-(benchmark, model) F1 and turns/ex across all four benchmarks. On the three multi-hop benchmarks, both generators shrink the action budget under telemetry while F1 holds or improves, with one Mistral-24B tradeoff on HotpotQA (F1=2.7, turns/ex=2.61). Benchmark Model F1 Turns -tel +tel HotpotQA Mistral-24B 67.9 65.2 2.7 2.61 Qwen3-32B 71.1 72.5 +1.4 1.26 2Wiki MuSiQue WiTQA Mistral-24B 68.0 70.8 +2.8 0.60 Qwen3-32B 66.0 69.7 +3.7 1.87 Mistral-24B 32.7 34.7 +2.0 +0.23 Qwen3-32B 42.6 42.4 0.2 0.64 Mistral-24B 87.5 89.2 +1.7 +0.30 Qwen3-32B 82.2 86.9 +4.7 +0.25 Table 1: Cross-benchmark results; WiTQA adds reranker over BM25; = CrossEncoder with telemetry(+tel) without telemetry(-tel) ( higher is better). Turns reports the in agent turns per example. On WiTQA, the same prompt does the opposite: the retrieval rate rises and F1 improves (Qwen332B F1=+4.7). This indicates that, when given telemetry signals, the agent is able to adapt to the prevalent failure mode (over-retrieval or parametric over-trust).

sections.title: 4.2 Portability
sections.content: Setup. To test whether the telemetry signal transfers across agent loops without retraining, we evaluate five published adaptive-retrieval frameworks, each with their original prompts and termination conditions. Each framework has its own gate for performing retrieval, i.e., per turn rule that decides whether to retrieve or not. We evaluate five frameworks that trigger retrieval based on an uncertainty signal. Per framework, baseline uncertainty and adaptive retrieval details are in Appendix E.4. We augment each baseline with CALVERT by replacing its native retrieval gate with DINCO confidence scores, inserted into the prompt. The baseline keeps its original gate; thus, DINCO scores the same candidate answers, and only the uncertainty signal and how it is used change. For TARG and SeaKR, we score the closed book questionanswer pair using the majority sampled answer. Verify-and-Edit scores the current subquestion and majority-sampled answer, while Self-Ask scores the current question and running answer. Results. Table 2 reports per-framework EM, F1, and total change in agent actions. Exposing the telemetry signal at each gate decision boosts F1 on four of the five frameworks; SUGARs F1 accuracy is essentially unchanged, but its retrieval cost falls by 34 retrieval calls. EM F1 Framework -tel +tel -tel +tel Act. V&E TARG SeaKR Self-Ask SUGAR 30.0 31.0 +1.0 38.8 41.0 +2.2 +22 33.0 47.0 +14.0 45.1 60.5 +15.4 +36 39.0 47.0 +8.0 51.0 58.7 +7.8 +31 43.0 47.0 +4.0 60.3 61.7 +1.4 05 50.0 46.0 4.0 61.3 60.3 1.0 34 Table 2: Framework portability (N =100 paired, HotpotQA, Qwen3-32B). The +tel column substitutes DINCO for each frameworks gate. Act. is the change in total agent actions.

sections.title: 4.3 Training-time Methods
sections.content: We next ask whether the agent can better learn to choose between the four actions based on outcome feedback. Following Search-R1 (Jin et al., 2025), we use GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) with verifiable rule based reward to train models for knowledgeintensive QA. Setup. We use GRPO to train LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) adapter (rank 16) on the per-turn agent via Tinker (Thinking Machines Lab, 2026), with all the other components frozen. We apply this recipe to dense Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-30B-A3B Mixtureof-Experts model on 1,600-question training pool drawn from the HotpotQA distractor dev set and evaluate it on 200-question held out set. We also evaluate the same recipe under telemetry-free prompt, thereby ablating the telemetry signal while keeping everything else identical. Hyperparameters are given in Appendix E.1. Each rollout is rewarded by the final answer quality minus the action cost. Valid rollouts receive token level F1 against the gold answer, plus small exact-match bonus, and incur penalties for extra turns and costly actions: retrieval/refinement, decomposition, or malformed actions. Following GRPO, we sample four rollouts per question and reward policies that outperform the group average while using fewer, cheaper actions. See Appendix E.1 for the full formulation. Results. Table 3 reports EM and F1 at step 100. Telemetry-aware GRPO improves Qwen3-8B by +9.0% EM and Qwen3-30B-A3B by +6.5% EM over their untrained baselines. The paired ablation isolates telemetry as the active ingreditelemetry-aware GRPO exceeds telemetryent: free GRPO by +7.0% EM / +5.9% F1 on 8B and +4.0% EM / +3.3% F1 on 30B-A3B, while telemetry-free GRPO barely improves. Qwen3-8B 30B-A3B Condition EM EM F1 Untrained (-tel) 26.0 38.6 28.5 39.2 GRPO (-tel) 28.0 39.7 31.0 40.8 GRPO (+tel) 35.0 45.6 35.0 44.1 Table 3: GRPO-trained policy at 100 steps results on 200 held out HotpotQA-distractor dev questions. Telemetry aware GRPO outperforms telemetry-free GRPO on both model bases. Action mean DINCO mean gmin commit 577 retrieve 0.98 0.59 0.89 0.33 Table 4: In HotpotQA telemetry run, Qwen3-32B commits when confidence and grounding are high, but retrieves when confidence is low and grounding is weak or absent.

sections.title: 4.4 Analysis
sections.content: Telemetry gains grow with question difficulty. Telemetry routes the agents action budget toward questions where parametric memory and shallow retrieval fail. Fig. 3 stratifies per-question F1 on Qwen3-32B: the HotpotQA gain grows monotonically with hop count, and the WiTQA gain concentrates on tail-popularity subjects while popular subjects are untouched. Both telemetry signals are essential for better action choices. Table 4 groups per-turn telemetry by the Qwen3-32B agents chosen action: commit correlates with high confidence and high grounding, while retrieve correlates with low confidence and low or absent grounding. This suggests that confidence tracks the agents propensity to commit, and grounding tracks commit acceptability. Prompt-only telemetry depends on model scale. Exposing the signal to frozen agent helps only at sufficient scale: on smaller Qwen3-8B base, the prompt-only setting underperforms its no-telemetry baseline, indicating that the agent does not yet act productively on the signal it is shown. However, we find that this can be mitigated via training, i.e., in Table 3.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: Across prompt-only, framework-substitution, and GRPO training settings, CALVERT improves the accuracy-cost tradeoff, reducing redundant actions and encouraging retrieval when appropriate. These gains show that calibrated telemetry provides simple, framework-agnostic mechanism for better agent control.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: By design, the CALVERT agent commits as soon as it finds sufficient answer, which suits single answer multi-hop QA (the setting of all four benchmarks we evaluate) but not answer recall tasks, where the gold answer must contain every relevant item (Zhu et al., 2024; Amouyal et al., 2023; Min et al., 2020). This failure mode stems from our commit centric action vocabulary rather than from the telemetry signal. Hence, accumulating complete answer set before terminating is signal agnostic extension we leave to future work.

sections.title: A Calibration scores of the telemetry
sections.content: signals For the telemetry signals to be usable in our framework, we make sure that our signals are calibrated by evaluating our confidence and evidencegrounding scoring methods on their task-specific benchmarks. For DINCO confidence signals, we score Qwen3-32B on closed-book questions from random =300 sample (seed=42) of the TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017) dataset validation split. We choose TriviaQA for this evaluation as its data distribution closely matches the closed-book knowledge regime in which DINCO operates within our framework. For MiniCheck, we draw 300 random (document, claim, label) examples from the LLM-AggreFact dataset, which has aggregated data points from multiple datasets used for the evaluation of grounded factuality of models. We use LLM-AggreFact for evaluation as its distribution matches our intended use case of evaluating the evidence grounding of model claims against retrieved passages. We report ECE-15, Brier, and AUROC scores with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals over 1,000 resamples. We report our calibration results in Table 5 and reliability plots in Fig. 2 . DINCO clears the standard < 0.10 ECE-15 bar, while MiniCheck achieves near-perfect discrimination with an AUROC of 0.95. Through these results, we show that the signals are usable as telemetry in our agent loop. Figure 2: Reliability diagrams for the two telemetry signals. Top: empirical accuracy per confidence bin, with the dashed line marking perfect calibration. Bottom: per-bin sample counts . Left (MiniCheck on AggreFact-RAG): the well populated high-confidence region exists at or above the diagonal, indicating MiniCheck to be confident for correctly grounded answers. Right (DINCO on TriviaQA) bars closely track the diagonal across the well-populated region, yielding strong calibration. Metric MiniCheck AGGREFACT-RAG DINCO TRIVIAQA/Qwen3-32B 0.139 [0.119, 0.189] ECE-15 0.098 [0.072, 0.127] Brier AUROC 0.948 [0.913, 0.976] 0.094 [0.072, 0.143] 0.150 [0.130, 0.173] 0.844 [0.792, 0.888] Table 5: Calibration metrics for the two telemetry signals on populations matching CALVERTs regime.

sections.title: B CIs Across Benchmarks
sections.content: For our prompt-only evaluation of CALVERT, we report results on = 300 samples evaluated with single seed value in Table 1. To evaluate whether our setup is robust across varying difficulty levels and question types, we perform test comparing the +tel and -tel settings on the same question pairs, and report the 95% confidence intervals for each test in Table 6. For each prompt-only experiment, we use the paired +tel and -tel answers and draw nboot = 10,000 paired index resamples from our = 300 dev split to compute the confidence intervals. We observe the same F1 improvements and shift in agent action turns in our bootstrap experiment as in the seed run reported in Table 1.

sections.title: C FLOP analysis of prompt only setups
sections.content: We analyze the computational overhead in TFLOPs of CALVERTs telemetry addition computed for verbalized confidence metrics using DINCO and grounding confidence scores using MiniCheck. We use torch flop counter to compute the TFLOPs consumed in the tel and notel settings by Qwen3-32B and Mistral-24B on two benchmarks. We report our results on 300 samples average TFLOPs in Table 7.

sections.title: D Additional Analysis
sections.content: Telemetry as Action Router. Telemetry, when exposed to harder questions, re-allocates the agents action budget against the prevailing failure mode rather than uniformly reducing search. On multihop benchmarks, adding telemetry shrinks the budget  (Table 1)  ; on WiTQA, it expands retrieval for tail entities.  (Fig. 3)

sections.title: E Implementation Details
sections.content: The prompt-level experiments use two open-weight dense generators, Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct (Mistral AI, 2025) and Qwen3-32B in non-thinking mode (Yang et al., 2025); the training-time experiments use Qwen3-8B and the Qwen3-30B-A3B Mixture-of-Experts model from the same family (Yang et al., 2025). The grounding verifier is fixed to Bespoke-MiniCheck-7B (Tang et al., 2024) across all cells. All generators are served with vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) on NVIDIA GH200 nodes. GRPO training runs on the Tinker training service. Benchmark Model F1 100 (95% CI) Turns (95% CI) HotpotQA Mistral-24B 2.7 [5.37, 0.06] 2.61 [3.01, 2.24] Qwen3-32B +1.4 [0.74, +3.55] 1.26 [1.52, 0.99] 2Wiki MuSiQue WiTQA Mistral-24B +2.8 [0.63, +6.11] 0.60 [0.89, 0.31] Qwen3-32B +3.7 [+1.01, +6.58] 1.87 [2.16, 1.59] Mistral-24B +2.0 [2.02, +5.94] +0.23 [0.11, +0.56] Qwen3-32B 0.2 [3.06, +2.59] 0.64 [1.00, 0.26] Mistral-24B +1.7 [0.48, +4.09] +0.30 [+0.25, +0.36] Qwen3-32B +4.7 [+1.20, +8.43] +0.25 [+0.20, +0.30] Table 6: Paired bootstrap 95% CIs on the eight experiments of Table 1. Bench Model F1 turns +DINCO tot % TFLOP Mistral-24B +2.8 0.60 2Wiki Qwen3-32B +3.7 1.87 2Wiki MuSiQue Mistral-24B +2.0 +0.23 MuSiQue Qwen3-32B 0.2 0. 290 +222 360 +159 258 +247 306 +200 GRPO Reward Formulation. For rollout τ with composer answer apred and gold agold, let F1(τ ) = F1(apred, agold) be the token based F1 reward and EM(τ ) = EM(apred, agold) be the EM reward. Table 7: Compute cost analysis of the prompt-level recipe, results showing deltas computed against +tel and -tel. F1 deltas reported out of 100. the agent

sections.title: A turn is one cycle of
sections.content: loop on which the agent emits one action from {commit, retrieve, refine, decompose}; we let nt count turns and nr, nf, nd count the non commit actions within them (commits incur only the base step cost). With n(τ ) = (nt, nr, nf, nd) and cost vector = (0.01, 0.02, 0.02, 0.04), the reward is Figure 3: Effect of telemetry across question difficulty and entity frequency. Deltas are computed against +tel and -tel for Qwen3-32B. (a) On HotpotQA, the F1 gain increases with hop count, suggesting that telemetry helps most on harder multi-hop questions. (b) WiTQA gain and retrieval rate increase concentrate on the rarest subject popularity buckets and diminish on popular entities. E.1 GRPO Training Details Architecture. The role-beam runner has four model-driven roles (planner, per-turn role agent, DINCO sampler, composer) plus hybrid BM25 + bge-base retriever (RRF k=60, top-3). We freeze the planner, DINCO, MiniCheck, and composer on local vLLM backend and train only the per-turn role agent as LoRA adapter (rank 16, lr 1104, AdamW) hosted on the Tinker training service. Composer and DINCO temperatures match the inference-time settings used elsewhere; the agent rolls out at =1.0 with top-p=0.95. Full GRPO training parameters are reported in Table 8. rϕ(τ ) = (cid:40) Rout(τ ), τ well-formed, 0.2, otherwise/malformed, (1) (cid:80) where Rout(τ ) = F1(τ ) + 0.1 EM(τ ) cn(τ ). For each prompt q, we sample G=4 rollouts {τi}G i=1 πθold( q) and form group-relative advantages ˆAi = rϕ(τi) 1 rϕ(τj), following GRPO. Training Set. We GRPO train on 1,600-question pool drawn from the HotpotQA distractor dev split (dev[0:1600]) rather than the official train split. This is difficulty driven choice: the train split is dominated by easy and medium questions, whereas the released dev split is entirely verified hard questions; Yang et al., 2018). Our policy learns when to retrieve, refine, decompose, or commit from verifier telemetry, and this action choice only matters on genuinely hard multi-hop questions. Training on the mixed train split would spend most updates on questions the frozen agent already resolves in one or two turns, yielding near zero gradients. We therefore treat this run as controlled hard regime ablation. E.2 Evaluation on Closed Source Models Table 9 reports results for gpt-4o and gpt-4.1 under the +tel and -tel settings, showing that Hyperparameter Value Models and adapter Base models LoRA rank Optimizer (AdamW) Learning rate (β1, β2, ε) Weight decay Grad accumulation GRPO / PPO Group size Prompts / step Trajectories / step PPO epochs / batch PPO clip range Rollout Sampling Turn budget Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-30B-A3B 16 1 104 (no warmup/schedule) (0.9, 0.95, 1012) 0 none (1 step/update) 4 rollouts / prompt 8 32 1 [0.8, 1.2] =1.0, top-p=0.95 3 agent turns (first subgoal) Schedule and selection Training steps Train questions Passes over pool 200, checkpoints used in eval: 100 1,600 (dev[0:1600]) 1 (2008 draws) Table 8: GRPO training hyperparameters for the Tinker LoRA. Both bases use identical settings. EM F1 Model Dataset -tel +tel -tel +tel turns/ex gpt-4o HotpotQA 51.0 54.0 +3.0 63.8 65.8 +2.0 32.0 42.0 +10.0 36.2 49.4 +13.2 2Wiki gpt-4.1 HotpotQA 59.0 58.0 1.0 73.8 75.8 +2.0 65.0 68.0 +3.0 75.0 78.1 +3.1 2Wiki +0.02 +0.08 +0.17 0.09 Table 9: Closed weight model results the telemetry signal is not specific to open-weight backbones. We keep the evaluation to 100 random samples from the test set due to budget constraints. CALVERT raises F1 on both datasets for both models, with the largest gain on the more retrievalintensive 2Wiki (+13.2 F1 for gpt-4o, +3.1 for gpt-4.1) and smaller gains on HotpotQA (+2.0 F1 for both). The action turns per example barely move, and the only regression observed is 1.0 EM drop for gpt-4.1 on HotpotQA, where overall F1 still improves. This indicates that calibrated telemetry continues to guide action choice in agents even on stronger proprietary models. We limit our closed-source evaluation to older gpt variants because we are unable to evaluate newer closedsource models, which no longer expose top-token log probabilities through their API, requirement for the pseudo-beam search that DINCO uses to compute its calibrated confidence scores. E.3 Performance with Individual Telemetry We perform an ablation study to assess the performance of using just one of the telemetry scores employed in CALVERT at time. We report our results on the 2Wiki multi-hop dataset and on single-hop WiTQA with = 300, using the same seed as in Table 1. We primarily test two setups. In the DINCO-only experiment, DINCO scores are computed for the closed-book setting and passed as telemetry without any post-retrieval scoring. In the MiniCheck-only experiment, we remove the DINCO closed-book component, allowing the agent to decide what action to take on the closed-book subquestion and then passing grounding scores if retrieval actions are triggered. We report the individual component results alongside the baseline no-telemetry results and the full-telemetry results in Table 10. In the multi-hop setting, neither signal in isolation matches the two combined. DINCO alone is the most detrimental and inflates the number of turns, suggesting that it leads the agent to overthink and struggle to converge on an answer. Specifically, we see that without added grounding telemetry in the post-retrieval stage, the agent chose repeated decompose and refine actions until the action budget ran out, thereby leading the composer to give an ungrounded incorrect output at the end. MiniCheck alone recovers much of this lost performance with fewer turns; yet, it still trails the full combination (CALVERT), which attains the highest EM and F1 while requiring relatively fewer agent turns per example. On the single-hop WiTQA benchmark, by contrast, all telemetry configurations reach nearly the same accuracy, yielding only marginal gains over the no-telemetry baseline. We attribute this to WiTQA being single-hop factoid benchmark with single gold evidence pool, which makes the task substantially easier than the multi-hop, multi-document setting of 2Wiki. Taken together, these results indicate that CALVERT delivers effective action routing primarily when question demands more complex multi-hop reasoning and retrieval from multiple sources. E.4 Adaptive Retrieval Frameworks We test the portability of CALVERT on five Adaptive Retrieval Frameworks. TARG (Wang et al., 2026) reads prefix logit uncertainty, the mean per token entropy of short no context draft, and triggers retrieval when it exceeds threshold τ . TARG is threshold agnostic, so we calibrate τ using the quantile rule mentioned in TARG to the target retrieval rate of ρ = .4 making τ = 0.045. SeaKR (Yao et al., 2025) reads an internal-state signal, the regularized log-determinant of the Gram Dataset Config EM F1 turns 2Wiki WiTQA DINCO 34.2 39.2 MiniCheck 54.0 60.4 -tel 59.3 66.0 +tel 62.0 69.7 DINCO 88.0 89.2 MiniCheck 88.7 89.6 -tel 86.1 87.5 +tel 88.4 89. 8.44 6.04 7.36 5.49 1.42 1.59 1.19 1.49 Table 10: Results (N =300). 2Wiki uses Qwen3-32B; WiTQA uses Mistral-24B. EM and F1 in %. tel uses both signals. matrix of last-token hidden states across = 20 stochastic generations at mid-network layer, and retrieves when this dispersion exceeds an operating point δ = 6. SUGAR (Zubkova et al., 2025) computes semantic-entropy signal, the entropy over clusters of samples grouped by bidirectional entailment, and splits it with two thresholds into no-, single-, or multi-step retrieval decision. We use the same quantile rule here to get the thresholds to be τlo = .81 and τhi = 1.0 . Verify-andEdit (Zhao et al., 2023) samples = 5 reasoning chains and verifies-then-retrieves when fewer than N/2 of them agree. Self-Ask (Press et al., 2023) uses no scalar gate; it iteratively emits follow-up subquestions, retrieving for each until it self-emits final answer.

sections.title: F Prompts
sections.content: F.1 Role-beam agent system prompt (telemetry) includes the confidence reading The prompt DINCO confidence (final_conf). Following Section 3, this is the equal-weight mean of the two self-confidence components, FINAL_CONF := 1 2 (NVC + SC) [0, 1], (2) and it is reported as mean DINCO in Table 4, averaged over the turns on which the agent took each action. The agent sees both the components (NVC, SC) and this summary; final_conf adds no information beyond them and is shown only so the policy has one calibrated reading without recombining the parts. The grounding family (gmean, gmin) is reported separately. You are retrieval policy controller for multi-hop question answering. You are processing one subquestion at time as part of answering larger multi-hop question. After each action you take, the system computes numerical telemetry from confidence and grounding models and shows it to you. You must decide what to do next. ## Available Actions Return STRICT JSON with exactly one action: When you commit, you MUST include an "answer" field with your final answer string. Commits without an answer field are invalid. {"action": "commit", "answer": "final answer string", "analysis": "your analysis of the telemetry", "reason": "why you are committing"} Accept the current answer. Use when confidence is high AND the answer is wellgrounded in evidence (or when you are confident the answer is correct from parametric knowledge for simple factoid questions). {"action": "retrieve", "query": "optional search query", "analysis": "your analysis of the telemetry", "reason": "why you need more evidence"} Search for more evidence passages. The query defaults to the subquestion text if omitted. Use when confidence is low or grounding is insufficient. {"action": "refine", "analysis": "your analysis of the telemetry", "reason": "why refinement would help"} Re-generate an answer using the same evidence with refinement prompt. Use when grounding scores suggest the evidence is adequate but the answer or its claims are poorly formulated. {"action": "decompose", "analysis": "your analysis of the telemetry", "reason": "why this question needs decomposition"} Give up on this subquestion and break it into smaller pieces. Use as last resort when retrieval and refinement have not helped. ## Telemetry Signals You will receive these numerical signals. Use them to inform your decision: - DINCO confidence (final_conf): Combined NVC + self-consistency score in [0,1]. Higher means the model is more internally consistent about the answer. - NVC (normalized verbal confidence): Verbal confidence normalized by how contradictory the alternative beam candidates are. - SC confidence: Self-consistency -- what fraction of independent samples agree with the main answer. - MiniCheck grounding (g_mean, g_min): How well the answer's support claims are grounded in retrieved evidence. Only available after retrieval. - Per-claim support scores: Individual grounding probability for each support claim. ## Decision Principles 1. Think before acting. Explain your reasoning in the "analysis" field before choosing an action. 2. An answer without supporting evidence is risky for non-trivial questions. 3. Diminishing returns. If multiple retrievals haven't helped, decomposition may be the right move. 4. Budget awareness. You have limited number of turns. Return STRICT JSON only. No markdown, no extra text outside the JSON object. F.2 Role-beam per-turn prompt Accept the current answer. Use when you believe the answer is correct. {"action": "retrieve", "query": "optional search query", "analysis": "your reasoning", " reason": "why you need more evidence"} Search for more evidence passages. The query defaults to the subquestion text if omitted. {"action": "refine", "analysis": "your reasoning", "reason": "why refinement would help"} Re-generate an answer using the same evidence with refinement prompt. {"action": "decompose", "analysis": "your reasoning", "reason": "why this question needs decomposition"} Give up on this subquestion and break it into smaller pieces. Use as last resort. ## Turn {turn}/{max_turns} -- Subquestion: "{ subquestion}" ## Decision Principles ### Original Multi-Hop Question {original_question} ### Current State - Current answer: "{answer}" - Answer source: {source} - Has dependency memory from earlier subquestions: {has_dependency_memory} - Evidence passages retrieved so far: { n_passages} ### Closed-Book Confidence Telemetry {closed_book_confidence_block} ### Grounding Telemetry (MiniCheck) {grounding_block} ### Action History {action_history_block} ### Budget {budget_remaining} turns remaining. Choose your next action wisely. Return STRICT JSON only: {"action": "...", " analysis": "...", "reason": "..."} F.3 No-telemetry ablation system prompt You are retrieval policy controller for multi-hop question answering. You are processing one subquestion at time as part of answering larger multi-hop question. After each action you take, the system updates the state and you must decide what to do next. ## Available Actions Return STRICT JSON with exactly one action: {"action": "commit", "analysis": "your reasoning", "reason": "why you are committing"} 1. Think before acting. Explain your reasoning in the "analysis" field before choosing an action. 2. An answer without supporting evidence is risky for non-trivial questions. 3. Diminishing returns. If multiple retrievals haven't helped, decomposition may be the right move. 4. Budget awareness. You have limited number of turns. Return STRICT JSON only. No markdown, no extra text outside the JSON object. Full Example: agent actions flip upon exposed telemetry We show multi-hop question example evaluated on Qwen3-32B where exposing the telemetry flips the outcome. Without telemetry, the agent commits to the wrong subquestion answer, thereby making the composer emit the incorrect final answer. In the case where telemetry is exposed to the agent, it decides to take the correct actions to retrieve more evidence to converge on the correct answer. The shared hop represents the subquestion for which both agents committed to the right answer. The fact shows the supporting fact that influenced the agent decision. (MuSiQue): Who played the girlfriend of the actor who played Marlene McFly in Back to the Future? GOLD: Claudia Wells shared hop: "Who played Marlene McFly?" -> " Michael J. Fox" ================ -tel exposed-> "Elisabeth Shue" [WRONG] sg2 "Who played the girlfriend of Michael J. No agent telemetry = Fox?" t1 retrieve t2 obs={answer:"Molly Ringwald"} act= obs={answer:"Elisabeth Shue"} act= # commits first commit -> "Elisabeth Shue" plausible candidate fact: "Elisabeth Shue played Jennifer Parker in Back to the Future Part II." COMPOSER -> "Elisabeth Shue" [WRONG] "Who played the girlfriend of Michael J. ================ +tel -> telemetry signals exposed [CORRECT] =========================== sg2 Fox?" t1 act=retrieve t2 obs={answer:"Lea Thompson", g_min:0.35} obs={answer:"Molly Ringwald", dinco:0.22} act=refine searching # low grounding -> keep t3 obs={answer:"Lea Thompson", g_min:0.35} act=retrieve t4 obs={answer:"Claudia Wells", g_min:0.93} act=commit -> "Claudia Wells" fact: "Jennifer Parker was played by Claudia Wells in Back to the Future." COMPOSER -> "Claudia Wells" [CORRECT]
